lesson.menu_desc: Disagreeing politely with concession linkers -- 35 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 12 -- Diplomatic Disagreement
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Pricila Adamo | Aula 12 -- Diplomatic Disagreement

reading.rtitle: The Art of Disagreeing Without Falling Out
paras: Few skills travel as well as the ability to disagree gracefully. Whereas a clumsy objection can sour an entire evening with new acquaintances, a diplomatic one can deepen the conversation. The secret, seasoned travelers say, is not to win the argument but to keep the door open. Although you may be certain the other person is wrong, you start by signalling that you see where they are coming from -- and only then do you gently push back.

Notice the shape of a graceful disagreement. First comes the concession: a phrase that grants the other side a fair point. Even though you do not fully agree, you concede whatever is true in their view. Next comes the pivot, often carried by a small but powerful word -- although, whereas, while -- that turns the sentence toward your own position without sounding combative. While they admire the museums, you may take issue with the queues; while you both want a good trip, you simply find common ground in different places.

The reward is real. People who learn to be at odds without being unpleasant rarely end a discussion as enemies. They beg to differ, they meet halfway where they can, and when no agreement is possible they happily agree to disagree. With all due respect, the writer concludes, the traveler who masters this quiet art will never run short of dinner companions, whereas the one who must always be right will soon be dining alone.
reading.source: Adapted from a travel and culture column, The Wanderer Review

gist.prompt: What is the writer's main point about disagreeing while traveling?
choices: You should avoid disagreeing with new acquaintances at all costs.
Disagreeing gracefully -- conceding a fair point, then pivoting politely -- keeps the conversation open and the friendships intact.
The most important thing in any discussion is to win the argument and prove the other person wrong.

items: The writer says the goal of a good disagreement is to win the argument.
f
The secret is not to win but to keep the door open.
A graceful disagreement often begins with a concession that grants a fair point.
t
First comes the concession: a phrase that grants the other side a fair point.
Words like although, whereas and while help you pivot toward your own view politely.
t
That small but powerful word turns the sentence toward your own position without sounding combative.
The writer believes people who must always be right make the best dinner companions.
f
The one who must always be right will soon be dining alone.

matching.title: Match each expression to its meaning
defs: an area where two people can agree
to argue an opposing view just to test an idea
to gently resist or challenge an idea
to make a compromise where both sides give a little
to admit that part of what someone says is true

items: Scenario 1
A fellow traveler insists the beach town is overrated and you should skip it. You loved it. React diplomatically -- concede something, then push back.
Scenario 2
A friend wants to spend the whole trip in museums; you would rather explore the markets. Find common ground and suggest meeting halfway.
Scenario 3
Someone at dinner claims local food is always too spicy for foreigners. You take issue with that. Disagree politely, with all due respect.
Scenario 4
A guide says public transport is too confusing for visitors. You beg to differ. Concede a fair point, then offer your counterargument.

rephrase.title: Join the two ideas into one sentence using the linker in brackets
items: I see your point. I still think the markets are worth a visit.
although
You love the quiet villages. I prefer the lively cities.
whereas
The tour was expensive. It was absolutely worth it.
even though
You enjoy planning every detail. I like to leave room for surprises.
while
She conceded the hotel was noisy. She refused to change it.
although

answerkey.title: Reveal the rephrase answer key
list: Although I see your point, I still think the markets are worth a visit.
You love the quiet villages, whereas I prefer the lively cities.
Even though the tour was expensive, it was absolutely worth it.
You enjoy planning every detail, while I like to leave room for surprises.
Although she conceded the hotel was noisy, she refused to change it.
answerkey.note: Form check: although / even though / while / whereas join two CLAUSES (each with its own subject + verb). When the linker starts the sentence, put a comma before the main clause; when it joins in the middle, the comma is optional, though a comma before whereas / while for contrast is common.

vocabnote.text: Watch the structure: although / even though / while + a full clause ("Although the trip was long, we loved it"). But despite / in spite of + a noun or -ing ("Despite the long trip, we loved it" / "In spite of being tired..."). Never say "although the long trip" or "despite it was long".

listenings.text: Honestly, I used to dread disagreeing with people I had just met on a trip. I was so afraid of sounding rude that I would just nod and say nothing, even though I clearly thought otherwise. Then a friend taught me a simple trick. You start by conceding something true. You say, that is a fair point, or I can absolutely see where you are coming from. Only then do you gently push back with your own view. The little word in the middle does the work. Although I agree the old town is beautiful, I would rather spend my day by the sea. Whereas before I stayed silent, now I beg to differ in a warm way, and people actually enjoy the conversation more. We rarely end up enemies. Usually we find some common ground, and when we cannot, we simply agree to disagree and move on to the next glass of wine.

Here is something I learned the hard way as a tour guide. Travelers do not really want a guide who is always right. They want one who can disagree gracefully. I remember a guest who was convinced our coastal route was a waste of time. With all due respect, I told him, I take issue with that, although I completely understand why a busy person would skip it. Then I offered my counterargument. Whereas the city is crowded all summer, the coast is calm and the light at sunset is extraordinary. He still played devil's advocate for a while, pushing back just to test me. But by the end, we had met halfway. He kept his morning in the museums, while I won him over to an afternoon by the water. He later told me it was the best part of his trip. Being at odds, it turns out, is not a problem, as long as you stay kind.

extra_audio.text: First, they realised they were at odds about the whole itinerary. Although she made a fair point about the museums, Pricila begged to differ. While her friend pushed back, Pricila conceded that the old town was lovely. They looked for common ground and finally met halfway. In the end, with all due respect, they simply agreed to disagree.
